content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 867 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance Administration Legal Enforcement Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5660 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-6444

May 14, 1982

Mr. Joseph E. Baird, Treasurer
Committee Against Government Excess
15040 Corona Del Mar
Pacific Palisades, CA 90272

No. A-82-096

Dear Mr. Baird:

This letter will confirm my recent telephone conversation in which I provided you with advice as to how to report certain campaign expenditures made in connection with advertisements against Proposition 9 or the Peripheral Canal. Although you did not solicit the Commission's advice, we contacted you in order to make sure that campaign expenditures involving advertisements against Proposition 9 or the Peripheral Canal were reported accurately. To date, we have received several inquiries regarding this matter. Our hope was that by providing you with guidance at this time, we could ensure that the public's interest in full disclosure was fully served and avoid complaints of misreporting.

The facts, as I understand them, are as follows:

As part of his campaign for Governor, Mr. Curb paid for the production of a television advertisement which featured him raising questions about the Peripheral Canal. Curb's committee then paid to have this advertisement aired on various television stations.

Subsequently, this advertisement was provided to the Committee Against Government Excess (CAGE) which paid to have the advertisement aired as part of its campaign against the Peripheral Canal.

At approximately the same time, Mr. Cory arranged for the writing and production of an advertisement in opposition to the Peripheral Canal. All of the production and airing costs were paid for by CAGE. At the time of these transactions, Curb and Cory were active candidates for state office.

Mr. Joseph E. Baird                -2-                May 14, 1982


     You informed the Commission that your purpose in airing
advertisements featuring Curb and Cory was to enlist the
assistance of the most persuasive Republican and Democratic
spokesmen in opposition to the Peripheral Canal. You stated
that any benefit to their respective candidacies was incidental
to your primary purpose of defeating the Peripheral Canal
measure.

     Based on our analysis of the information available to
us, we conclude that Curb's committee and CAGE have made
mutually beneficial in-kind contributions to each other.
In addition, CAGE has made an in-kind contribution to Cory.
First, the Curb committee made an in-kind contribution to
CAGE when they provided CAGE with a copy of the advertise-
ment. This advertisement is a product, like a typewriter or
an automobile, which costs money to purchase on the open
market.

     Second, CAGE made in-kind contributions to Curb and
Cory when it paid for the advertisements to be aired on
television stations throughout the State. This constitutes
an in-kind contribution to them because they did not have to
pay to have their messages aired throughout the State at a
time when they were active candidates for state office.
Previously, the Commission has advised candidates that pay-
ments made in connection with a TV advertisement or a
campaign mailing are in-kind contributions to the candidate
if the candidate had direction or control over the contents
of the communication and the communication was made in the
jurisdiction subject to the candidacy.

     Lastly, CAGE made an in-kind contribution to Cory when
it paid the production costs for the advertisement.

     The fact that your primary purpose in making the Curb
and Cory advertisements may have been to defeat the Peripheral
Canal is not controlling; rather the dispositive factor in
our determination that CAGE has made an in-kind contribution
to Curb and Cory is the fact that they exercised direction
and control over the contents of the communication during
a time in which they were active candidates for state office.

     Having concluded that these activities constitute in-
kind contributions, we turn to the question of how they
should be reported on campaign statements. Since these
contributions mutually benefited either Curb or Cory and
CAGE, we think either of two alternative ways of reporting
would be satisfactory. If you wish, you may divide the

Mr. Joseph E. Baird                    -3-                    May 14, 1982


relevant expenditures apportioning half of the benefit to
CAGE and half of the benefit to Curb or Cory.  Thus, half
of the production cost of the Curb advertisement would be
an in-kind contribution from the Curb committee to CAGE.
Similarly, half of the payments made in connection with
airing both Curb and Cory advertisements would be in-kind
contributions from CAGE to each of them respectively.  In
addition, half of the production costs for the Cory advertise-
ment would be an in-kind contribution to Cory.  These
transactions should be noted on the appropriate in-kind
contribution, expenditure, and allocation schedules.

     Alternatively, you may avoid the issue of "apportion-
ment" entirely be reporting the full amount of these expend-
itures on the allocation and in-kind contribution schedules
with a notation that they mutually benefited either Curb
or Cory and CAGE.  For example, the full amount of payments
made in connection with airing the Curb advertisement would
appear on CAGE's allocation schedule with a notation that
these costs were incurred in connection with airing an
advertisement on the Peripheral Canal featuring Curb.

     In addition to reporting these matters on the allocation
schedule, each payment made in connection with these transactions
should be clearly identified as benefiting both CAGE and Curb
or Cory on the expenditure schedules.

     In light of the pendency of the election, we request
that any amendments to previous campaign statements which
are necessitated by this advice be filed as quickly as
practicable.

     If we may be of further assistance, please do not
hesitate to contact us.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        Ted Prim
                                        Executive Director

TP:cs

sections.question_synthetic: How should in-kind contributions be reported when a political committee (CAGE) airs advertisements featuring active candidates (Curb and Cory) for a ballot measure campaign, where the candidates' campaigns and the committee mutually benefit from the arrangement?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that mutual in-kind contributions were made between Curb's committee and CAGE, and between CAGE and Cory. These contributions must be reported either by apportioning half the benefit to each party or by reporting the full amount on allocation and in-kind contribution schedules with clear notation of mutual benefit.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: No formally labeled QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections present; question and conclusion synthesized from letter content. Document marked 'SUPERSEDED IN PART' at top. Letter is unsolicited advice provided by FPPC to clarify reporting for multiple similar inquiries received. Strong extraction confidence despite lack of section headers due to clear factual recitation and analysis.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: How should in-kind contributions be reported when a political committee (CAGE) airs advertisements featuring active candidates (Curb and Cory) for a ballot measure campaign, where the candidates' campaigns and the committee mutually benefit from the arrangement?

CONCLUSION: The FPPC concluded that mutual in-kind contributions were made between Curb's committee and CAGE, and between CAGE and Cory. These contributions must be reported either by apportioning half the benefit to each party or by reporting the full amount on allocation and in-kind contribution schedules with clear notation of mutual benefit.

embedding.summary: FPPC advises the Committee Against Government Excess (CAGE) on reporting requirements for in-kind contributions arising from advertisements against the Peripheral Canal that featured active gubernatorial candidates Curb and Cory, providing two alternative reporting methodologies.
